text: This is the case of the left upper lobectomy.
The Maryland Dissector is quick and easy to dissect the pleura over the anterior hilum, allowing access to the pulmonary artery and superior pulmonary vein.

The ability to articulate the fenestrated forceps to a 90 degree angle in both directions enables dissection around the superior pulmonary vein with far more precision than a standard VATS instrument.
The blunt tip minimizes the risk of damage to the vessels.
Again, you can see how easily and efficiently it is to swap to the stapling device through the sub-siphoid port as the surgeon is by the patient's side.
Next, we found some lymph nodes behind the superior pulmonary vein.
Again you can see how nicely the fenestrated forceps can be used to grab these lymph nodes at any angle without damaging them.
The bipolar mary lens are then used to dissect the lymph nodes out.
Getting these lymph nodes out in a VATS case would probably be a bit trickier as you can't get these perfect angles with traditional port instruments.
The next step was to dissect around the anterior segmental pulmonary artery branch.
We are alternating the Merrylands with a fenestrated forceps to get around this vessel, which worked really well.
Again, the angulation of the instruments is great for this situation.

You might think that using the Merrylands is potentially dangerous in this case because of their pointed tip, but actually the tactile feedback you get makes it possible to use them for careful dissections in places where the blood vessel is well presented.
Here you can see that we've created a nice path around the anterior segmental pulmonary artery branch, which can then easily be followed by the stapler.
